avoid: Replacing cuBLAS for the large W @ X GEMM (high risk, unlikely to beat cuBLAS).
Hardcoding hidden dimension d or relying on shape-specific tuning without dispatch.
Ignoring memory bandwidth and focusing only on launch overhead reduction.
evidence: Aggregate speedup 0.987 < 1.0, with per-shape speedups 0.985 (3584), 0.988 (4096), 0.987 (4608), all below baseline.
Correctness is perfect (max_abs_err=0.0, rel_l2_err=0.0), so performance regression is not due to numerical issues.
Baseline (torch) likely uses cuBLAS for the rank-16 matmuls, which is highly optimized even for small K=16.
Custom two-kernel approach writes intermediate C (16×d) to global memory and reads it back, adding bandwidth pressure.
Block size 256 may be suboptimal for K=16, leading to low occupancy or wasted threads.
Vector width 4 suggests float4 loads, but stores may not be vectorized, causing inefficient memory transactions.
recommended_actions: Profile the two custom kernels separately to pinpoint whether B.T @ X or Y += A @ C is the bottleneck.
Experiment with smaller block sizes (e.g., 128, 64) to improve occupancy for the thin K=16 dimension.
Ensure both loads and stores use float4 vectorization when d is divisible by 4.
Fuse the two rank-16 kernels into a single kernel that computes Y += A @ (B.T @ X) without materializing C, reducing global memory traffic.
Consider using shared memory to cache A and B tiles to reduce redundant global loads.
